text.title: code-review Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for a high-throughput, high-quality code review pipeline.

rule.statement: PR diff size MUST be under 400 changed lines (excluding generated files, lockfiles, snapshots). Larger PRs require a `large-pr-approved` label and a written rationale.
rule.rationale: Review quality drops sharply above ~400 lines (Cohen-Brown 2010; Cisco study). Splitting forces logical boundaries and faster turnaround.

rule.statement: Reviewers do not start until lint, types, tests, coverage gate, and security scan are all green. The PR template carries the link to the latest CI run.
rule.rationale: Reviewers should not spend cycles flagging issues automated gates catch. Red CI = the PR is not ready for review.

rule.statement: Reviewers focus on correctness (bugs, edge cases) and design (boundaries, naming, abstractions). Style / formatting issues are delegated to ruff / prettier / eslint. Subjective taste is out of scope.
rule.rationale: Style debates produce review-bottleneck. Automating style turns review into actual review.

rule.statement: An agentic pre-review pass (Claude Code / Cursor) runs BEFORE human review and posts a structured comment: (1) risk callouts, (2) test coverage delta, (3) detected anti-patterns. Human reviewer reads the structured comment first.
rule.rationale: Agentic pre-review is cheap and catches 60-70% of low-hanging issues (missing tests, unhandled errors, magic numbers) before a human spends a single minute.

rule.statement: Merge requires (a) all required checks green, (b) one human approval (two for `critical` modules), (c) all conversations resolved. Bots may auto-merge dependency bumps after a clean check run + 2-hour soak.
rule.rationale: Explicit merge policy prevents review-by-thumbs-up and ensures auto-merge stays scoped to safe categories.
